author: Adnan
caption:
One thing about our recent adventures in France has completely ruined for me is the idea of a “quick stop.”

You walk into a church for five minutes, look up once, and suddenly you’re standing there studying centuries-old stained glass like you have nowhere else to be.

Basilique Saint-Michel sits in one of Bordeaux’s oldest neighborhoods, shaped by merchants, artisans, sailors, immigration, conflict, and constant reinvention. It feels less like a monument and more like a building that has absorbed whatever the city threw at it.

That is probably why I liked it.

The windows are beautiful, but not in a neat, untouched way. They carry damage, replacement, restoration, and changing taste. Some things survived because people valued them. Some disappeared because people didn’t.

Which is a slightly uncomfortable reminder that history is not only shaped by what happened.

It is shaped by what someone decided was worth keeping. And what was worth throwing away.... Sigh.

#aazaadventures

author: Amy
caption:
They don’t make things like they used to…

#aazatravels

author: Amy
caption:
PURPLE: photos from Bordeaux’s daily farmer’s market, local street art, and stained glass from a cathedral’s window. 

#aazatravels

author: Amy
caption:
Toulouse surprised me with its beauty. All of these French cities seem to have a lovely old city center, but here it felt like the whole city was beautiful. It’s nicknamed La Ville Rose (“The Pink City”) because of the warm pink and terracotta bricks used throughout the city. I went out early one morning to take photos, and once the sunlight hit the brick buildings, they seemed to glow orange.

I wandered the streets and felt incredibly safe, even at 6:30 a.m. with my camera. There was a very large park (Jardin des Plantes) just half a block from our place. We visited it daily, enjoying its many benches, statues surrounded by lush flowers, shaded paths, and pedestrian bridges. If you read the kids’ blogs they really loved the Royal Garden section where there is a pond with ducks, ducklings, geese, bridges, and waterfalls. 

One day we painted in the park through a city-funded art program where anyone could stop by and try acrylic painting. We met a young Parisian woman whom Ayana quickly befriended. Another day, Ayana, Zarmina, and I brought our watercolors and painted together in the park. I’m a big fan of how the French use their public spaces. 

A church I had walked past that morning was one the kids wanted to explore later, Saint-Étienne Cathedral. It was awe-inspiring, with the stained glass casting rainbow-colored light across the floor and walls. I’m glad the kids recommended exploring it.

#aazatravels

author: Adnan
caption:
Last morning run in Toulouse. On our way to Bordeaux and looking forward to creating some rituals around our world schooling journey. I'll keep you updated on the next video.

#aazaadventures

author: Adnan
caption:
Really loved the Halle de la Machine in Toulouse. Highly recommend if you're in town. And make sure to stay for the dinner performance. We unfortunately missed that one but did see the Minotaur dance!

#aazaadventures

author: Adnan
caption:
Cancelled again. The heat waves are making the tracks too dangerous for so many trains to run on so about 10% off trains are cancelled. And the world is on fire.
I Didn't plan on around this morning but here it goes. Saw a waterfall. Hopefully Carcassonne tomorrow....

#aazaadventures

author: Adnan
caption:
Have you ever tames a monster? Or better yet, danced with one?

At @halledelamachine in Toulouse, the Minotaur doesn't just stomp across the stage. 

The minotaur Dances!

And then there's the woman, suspended high above him, floating, fearless, almost untouchable. Theovement is magical with her above and the minotaur below. (Neck did hurt watching the entire performance!)

It's a strange pairing. Beauty and brute force.
Control and surrender. 
Humanity and instinct.

Maybe that's the point.

Because that's what dance is all about. Challenging conceptions and preconceptions to get us to think through new possibilities where none may have existed before.

Capturing and releasing thoughts that are so fleeting you don't even know you're having them and then you realize, oh yes, I'm watching a performance. I'm not in it. This is not real. Or is it unreal?

We all carry a Minotaur inside us. Ego. Fear. Ambition. Rage. The parts we'd rather hide.or maybe I should say the parts I would hide, since this is my story... Then again, what's yours? 

What monsters lurk in your backyard? Don't tell me, show me. Or come visit Le Halle de la Machine in Toulouse. It's magical and the people (actors, scientists, dancers, performers) there are absolutely wonderful!

#aazaadventures

author: Adnan
caption:
Toulouse morning run Part deux.

Adding to the second half of the run and seeing more lovely skylights (as in pretty pink clouds in the sky).

#aazaadventures

author: Adnan
caption:
Toulouse Run Number 1. Part 1.
70 F / 20 C and humidity is 75%.
I definitely got a sweat going but it was lovely to see the Pink City in all its Glory. There were a lot of people out again. I'm imagining they've been up all night. I saw very few runners maybe three or four and most of them at the river.

#aazaadventures

author: Amy
caption:
BROWN: (mostly brown) I’ve got a new theme of door designs and door knobs. I thought Marseille had nice doors but we’re now in Toulouse and there’s an abundance of beautiful doors. I might stop every few feet! 

#aazatravels

author: Amy
caption:
I found out the largest annual photo festival in the world was happening an hour away in Arles (Les Rencontres d’Arles). I decided to go solo because the kids can’t last in the intense heat and humidity and I wanted to see all that I could. The festival has over 30 exhibitions around the historic town and many inside the older buildings. It’s quite a contrast to see contemporary photography and anti-colonial themes inside of these ancient places. 

Not every exhibit was indoors — there was a “flower power” one that had large format photos around a park. I wandered past an amazing outdoor market with beautiful vintage items. I learned about Ghana’s history of fighting for freedom through photos, took a look at 200 years of animal photography and its themes, and checked out the photos from a scientist who gathers water samples around Marseille and figured out a way to make a gel and photograph the bacterial colors. I went by  the Van Gogh gardens and saw the Roman ruins that are right in the center of the old town. 

I am really glad I made the trip there but my drive home was stressful - I’ve now learned never to get below 100k gas in a car here because the gas stations are few and far between; my credit cards don’t work in the machines (I thankfully had euros); and there are nine types of gas and it’s not uncommon to have to go to another station to get the right gas the car needs. But I made it back with 2% on my phone and I’m here to write about it. 😬😊

#aazatravels

author: Adnan
caption:
Toulouse!! Finally made it after the 6.5 trip in 3 trains and 2 transfers. Got to the Airbnb we're staying at and right before our host left she let us know there's no Wifi. And I've been going to all sorts of coffee shops asking if they have wifi and it's a no-go. So, since I do have quite a few meetings this coming week I'll have to explore the co-working setup out here. Fun times.

Also, glad that we're staying right near these incredible parks. Hoping to do some morning runs out here and I'll definitely be reporting back out. We've got a good 4-5 days here!

#aazaadventures

author: Adnan
caption:
CANCELLED! Our TGV tickets were cancelled a couple of days ago. Just out of the blue. Well, because of the heat, of course.... What does that mean? We get up at 5:30 to get to the train station at 7:00 am so that we can get to Toulouse by 1:30. It would have taken us about 2 hours by TGV but here we are with three trains, two transfers, and a boatload (trainload) of fun that awaits us. 

I guess taking early trains is better because the trains don't melt (well, not yet) at 7 am versus at 11 am.

Lesson learned.

##aazaadventures

author: Amy
caption:
TURQUOISE BLUE: My family knows that when I’m not right behind them on a walk, I’ve stopped to take a photo. 🩵

#aazatravels

author: Amy
caption:
I’m so impressed that this man can paint on the narrow sidewalk on a bridge where there is a lot of traffic. 

#aazatravels

author: Adnan
caption:
Last Run in Marseille at 5:30 a.m. Hot hot hot (85% humidity too) but glad that I got out again. Looking forward to some early morning runs and to-do's where it will actually be about 10 F / 4C cooler.

Got a headstand in around halfway through the run.

#aazaadventures

author: Adnan
caption:
So many folks come to Marseille and go to the Old Port, the Fort Saint Jean, etc, but the actual substance, the heart of the city is up the hill in Le Panier.

It’s the oldest quarter, but it doesn't function like a static museum. It's an art gallery. The environment is entirely fluid. Local artists constantly paint over old murals, so the streets look different every few weeks.

We spent the morning navigating the alleys, checked out the 17th-century courtyard at La Vieille Charité, and stopped in a quiet square for some yummy treats.

Highly recommend getting here early in the morning. There was almost no one here at 7a on July 14th!

#aazaadventures

author: Amy
caption:
Le Panier district was a wonderful surprise. I spent time researching where to visit in Marseille and surrounding areas, but I hadn’t paid much attention to this neighborhood until the other day. There have been so many places to see and things to do.

I picked the right day to visit. We went very early (by French summer standards) and on a holiday. I hadn’t expected to find such elaborate street art, with virtually every corner displaying some kind of creativity. The streets have the classic look and charm of a historic French neighborhood, narrow alleyways, colorful shutters, stone buildings, and lanterns, but they’re also filled with graffiti, stickers, mosaics, shells, and other artistic details.

Le Panier is the oldest district in Marseille, built on the site of the city’s ancient Greek origins (I was surprised to learn it was a Greek settlement first). For much of the 20th century it was a rougher, less expensive neighborhood, which attracted artists and craftspeople. Over decades art became a part of the neighborhood and more recently it’s become a favorite area in Marseille. With few people around and shops closed, it was much easier to photograph. I wrote about it the other day but reliving some photos today. 

#aazatravels

author: Adnan
caption:
Most people think Fort Saint-Jean was built to protect Marseille.

Only half true.

Louis XIV also used it to keep watch over the city after Marseille dared to challenge the crown. Imagine building a fortress that's aimed as much at your own people as at invading armies... Fun times.

Today, it's one of the best places to wander in Marseille. Well, if it not so ridiculously hot. Like today. It's just a balmy warmish 80F // 25C. But somewhat rainy.

If you visit Marseille, don't just walk across the bridge to MuCEM. Slow down and explore the fort itself. 

But for now we're headed to the museum...

#aazaadventures

author: Adnan
caption:
Oh, wonder of Wonders is when we visited Aix en Provence we also got the chance to visit this Harry Potter store. It's even more magical because the street looks like it could be straight out of Harry Potter as well.
#aazaadventures

author: Amy
caption:
ORANGE: Ayana asked me in an alley what my color was today. I look over and there is a bright orange umbrella, I turn a corner and there’s shiny bright orange decoration on a building, and mini orange tree and an orange store with orange candies inside, and here we are…

#aazatravels

author: Amy
caption:
Aix-en-Provence is so beautiful. We arrived early-ish and walked the streets. Everything seems picturesque. The first stop was Pâtisserie Béchard for the decant treats. The large Saturday market was in full swing nearby. It’s a very popular town. Aix was built on top of water so there are many, supposedly, around 100 fountains around town. We saw one being used to cool some wine bottles. There is a well known fountain that looks like a bush with plants growing in it. There was a Harry Potter store tucked in an alleyway, much to Ayana’s delight. We ended our time in a bookstore/cafe browsing the books and eating lunch. It inspired the kids to request a lot more books for their kindles - sorry to the bookstore as we’re traveling pretty lightly. Honestly, I’d love to come back and spend more time here - with less people and a bit cooler! 

#aazatravels

author: Adnan
caption:
Enjoy the lovely street performance of the incredible entertainers in Avignon. There is a festival there every year and it's preparing us for the fringe festival in Edinburgh next month!

I'll be sharing some more specific acts soon too.

#aazaadventures

author: Amy
caption:
YELLOW: We woke up early and roamed Le Panier district. It was surprisingly quiet until we confirmed with the one open coffee shop owner that it’s a holiday today. Tomorrow is the national holiday but perhaps people get both days off. Tonight there will be fireworks. It was so peaceful to walk the streets and check out the coolest street art area that I’ve seen, including alleys with actual art work hung up between beautiful green and flowering plants, decorated doors, and decorative fabrics hung where people have made a wish. I’ll be back to this area. Note that not all photos are from Le Panier but the majority. Can you see Adnan? 

#aazatravels

author: Amy
caption:
Calanques: The three hour boat ride along the Calanques National Park is really pretty and even reminded me of a boat ride we took in the Algarve region in Portugal. We went from Marseille to Cassis and back. The guide said the city has been here for 2,600 years (obviously not including the time before when the Casquer caves were used up to 33,000 year ago!). I was glad to take the early 9:30am tour because the weather was so hot, even with the boat moving and creating some wind. The captain drove the boat into one of the narrow limestone crevasses which was slightly alarming as the waves pushed us forward into a space too tight for the boat (photos 9 and 10). The scale of the cliffs is hard to capture. I’m glad we did the boat ride despite questioning the decision to do so the day before! Admittedly, a few of us felt a little sea sick part of the time. 

#aazatravels 

#

author: Adnan
caption:
Amazing walk through the Panier (basket?) district in Marseille. It's literally like walking through an art gallery outside. Today's a holiday right before the fete national and there was almost nobody on the streets at 7:00 a.m. What an incredible way to start the morning and even though the humidity was ridiculous and it was pretty hot, it being overcast made it a very pleasant walk.

I'll post more of the art later. This was just a cute little street with some lovely messages that I couldn't not share.

#aazaadventures

author: Adnan
caption:
I almost didn't go for a run this morning in Marseille.It's about 80°F / 25 C and the humidity is over 80%. As soon as I walked out I was blown away by the intense heat but I managed to go out for 4 miles / 6.5km and I'm happy that I did as today is probably going to be mostly a rest day for us. 

I do have a #headstandfail photo towards the end of the video. Check it out. Lmk what you think. 

#aazaadventures

author: Adnan
caption:
Such a wonderful feeling to visit the place I grew up. Also, I took it so much for granted. It's one of the loveliest (and poshest, from what I hear) neighborhoods around Paris. 
I have fond memories of that bakery as well!
#aazaadventures

author: Amy
caption:
RED: I picked red as soon as I saw the cute house with red and the red gate. Today we went to La Ciotot, about a 30 mins drive from where we are staying.  We started at the Park du Mugel and hopped in the water at the small pebbly beach. I walked around the botanical garden where the crickets make a strong buzzing sound. Sunday is the big market day with food and products. We had a tasty lunch at La Petite Creperie. We learned that most places finish up breakfast at 11am and close until noon for lunch. We happened to be looking at 11am! We met up with Rachel, who knew the assignment (red shirt!). 😁 Ayana took that photo 🙏. 

#aazatravels

author: Amy
caption:
This church, Notre Dame de la Guarde, sits at the top of Marseille and “watches over the city and its sailors.” There are strings of model boats hanging from the ceiling which have come from sailors grateful to the Virgin Mary for saving them from shipwrecks and storms. The outside of the church looks pretty but the inside was surprisingly bright and gold. The entire floor is covered in small mosaic stones. We took a little tourist train car up to the top but I was very impressed seeing people walking up to this place in midday humid heat. We walked down and even that was almost too hot! There is a view of all of Marseille from here. 

#aazatravels

author: Amy
caption:
GREEN: today’s color around Marseille picked by Ayana, photographed by me. What should I do tomorrow? 

#aazatravels

author: Adnan
caption:
In beautiful Aix en Provence we found a lovely bookshop/cafe called The Book Bar where @atbennington was represented! @kangaroorunrun book The Loneliness of Sonia and Sunny prominently displayed as well as Donna Tartt's the secret history.
#aazaadventures

author: Adnan
caption:
Yummy juice @sweetpanda_aix in Aix en Provence.
#aazaadventures

author: Adnan
caption:
Hot day for a boat ride hut was cool.wnough when we were moving! 
No voiceover on this one, enjoy the music. Enjoy the ride.
#aazaadventures

author: Amy
caption:
I had every intention to go to the Mucem, the well known museum right along the water. I walked towards the building and saw a bright kiosk “Les Billets” tickets! And purchased three for me and the kids. As we walked inside, I realized that I’m at a completely different museum! It was a “happy accident” as Adnan said. It was the Cosquer Caves, which took us to a life size replica of these caves that were discovered by Henri Cosquer in the 80’s by scuba diving. With zero expectations, we ended up having a great time. We learned a lot about these caves and the kids were really inspired by the experiential nature of the cave “ride” (like a Disney ride) that moved us past the stalagmites and chandelier formations and pointed out all the cool paintings and hand prints in the cave. These date back as far as 33,000 years ago! The glaciers held so much water back then that the caves were accessible for humans to walk into them. The water levels were 400 feet lower. The paintings show lots of extinct animals like the Megaloceros (Ayana’s standing under one). The cave wasn’t lived in but perhaps seen as a spiritual place. Post ride we saw the life sized animal replicas. There’s also a message about climate change - there was little change in our ocean levels for 6000 but that’s going to change rapidly as the glaciers begin to melt. The more the water levels rise, these paintings will be destroyed. 

We did meander over to the Mucem but the exhibits were going to be too much for one day. We instead walked through the cool building and over to the fort and jardin and then to a pedestrian bridge to a nice area for lunch. It was the hottest day yet so we’re kind of melting when a guy offered to take a photo of the three of us. 

#aazatravels

author: Adnan
caption:
Part 2 of the morning run brings you the opera, more questions about the three things french people love that go round and round (first hint/reveal is in the first video), the Abbey constructed in the 5th century (that 1600 years ago!), and the fort of Marseille. Enjoy.
#aazaadventures

author: Adnan
caption:
6:30 am morning run. Temp is a little higher, around 80F // 25C.
Featuring a lovely Beach, a park dedicated to resistance fighters in WWII, the old port (where's the new one?) and some cute streets downtown. Oh and the place where I took a wrong turn and almost had the cops arrest me.
#aazaadventures

author: Amy
caption:
We’re staying in Vallon des Auffes area in Marseille. It’s a little outside the city center but it’s very beautiful. It has a good energy. We took an evening walk and the warm light at sunset was so beautiful. The people on the rocks remind me of what would be seals in San Francisco! Evening walks along the main road feel a bit chaotic with cars, bikers, and the many runners dodging around us. 

#aazatravels

author: Amy
caption:
Cassis is a very cute town about 45 mins from Marseille. I heard a lot of English spoken, ie it’s popular with the tourists. I can see why. It has that quintessential French Rivera vibe. A million ice cream shops beckoning on every corner. There is a beach in the center of town with a literal sea of humanity. So many people on holiday on the sand and in the sea. We rented an umbrella and chairs - pricey but worth it - as the sand is a mix of pebbles, shale and sand. The water is refreshing albeit a touch unclean at the shore. I’d love to come back and explore more. Nearby is Calanques National park and there are beautiful limestone cliffs in the area. It felt a bit like stepping into a postcard. 

#aazatravels

author: Adnan
caption:
Paris has the Louvre, the Eiffel Tower, Notre Dame.... and then it's got....
The Marché aux Puces, where every object looks like it has seen seven lifetimes worth of events and is currently judging your clothing choices. I definitely felt judged.

Old signs, strange lamps, velvet chairs, cracked mirrors, toys from my childhood (just mine), vintage clothing (like REAL vintage), impossible chandeliers, and the occasional thing you’re not sure is art, plumbing, or could it be used as evidence in a court case?

This is my kind of place to hang, if it weren't so dang HOT! Well, it'll cool down. Eventually.... LeBigSigh.

#aazaadventures

author: Amy
caption:
Zarmina’s main goal this year is exploring/tasting different foods. She’s got a sweet tooth so it leans heavily towards dessert. We’ve had some tasty food so far. The chocolate mousse (in Paris) doesn’t look amazing but it was incredible. The pizza is from a local restaurant near our place and I hope to go back. It opened for dinner at 7pm and we luckily arrived soon after as it filled right up. Marseille is very crowded this time of year (as expected). I didn’t realize until later Ayana made her own mustache with ice cream. 

#aazatravels

author: Adnan
caption:
First run in Marseille not as early as I would like since jet lag is still a thing. Managed to get out by 7:00 a.m.
Lovely views of the coastline. Couple of beaches one rocky one sandy (fun name for a couple, no?) and of course the count of monte cristo Island.
#aazaadventures

author: Adnan
caption:
Monday 11 am - crowded beach. Summer time!!

#AAZAadventures

author: Adnan
caption:
Marseille is HOT right now but the walks along the water are spectacular. There were so many people on the beach on Sunday night but Monday morning only a few people were in the water, mostly snorkeling. Also, the Count of Monte Cristo took place on that fortress in the island you see!
#aazaadventures

author: Amy
caption:
Zarmina, Ayana, and I spent a relaxing few hours painting watercolor in the Tuileries garden. Khae, our teacher, is from Myanmar. I’m very impressed she came to France speaking neither French or English but now is fluent in both after four years. I found her class on Airbnb experiences. I hope to paint more as we move around, and this class inspired me. 

#aazatravels

author: Adnan
caption:
Got the chance to visit my old school Marymount international School used to be called Marymount School of Paris in the Neuilly area. So cool.to revisit after so many years. Also, Madeleine went to school.here too!
#aazaadventures

author: Amy
caption:
One weekday around lunch, we walked to a nearby park that was bustling with hundreds of people out and about - eating lunch in pairs on park benches, huddled in friend groups on picnic blankets, leisurely walking their dogs with friends, and enjoying the pleasant weather outside. We sat and observed while I had honey bees flying around in the wildflowers behind me. It was so great to see a beautiful park so well loved. 

#aazatravels

author: Amy
caption:
I loved Musée d’Orsay and the Renoir exhibit (maybe took too many photos of paintings!). I bought the kids audio guides and then had some FOMO and got one for myself. It made it much easier to absorb the information on so many paintings. The Renoir theme was about love and connections, and there were several paintings with women that could have been a celebration of same sex relationships (ahead of his time in the mid 1800’s). He also “eliminated gender domination” and showed happy bonds between men and women.

The Musée d’Orsay building is a piece of art itself as a former train station. The museum was created to bridge the gap between the older art at the Louvre and the modern art at the Pompidou Centre, showcasing the impressionist era. The view from the 5th floor is really stunning and looked absolutely surreal!

#aazatravels

author: Amy
caption:
The Arc de Triomphe, built over 30 years in the early 1800’s, is very impressive. Napoleon wanted it built to honor the French fighters post-Battle of Austerlitz, but he didn’t get to see it completed. His casket did ride through the Arc after it was completed. The eternal burning fire is checked on daily by the military to be rekindled. The roundabout is a bit chaotic. I’m thankful to not be driving here!

#aazatravels

author: Adnan
caption:
All the perspectives. 

#aazaadventures

author: Adnan
caption:
Renoir. At musée d'orsay. Go see it.
#aazaadventures

author: Amy
caption:
First stop in Paris, a corner bakery for croissants followed by an uphill walk to Sacre Coure. The street art is beautiful as well as the Parisian buildings and cobble stone streets at sunset. At Sacre Coure, there was live music floating up from different areas as we took in the view of the city. It felt surreal walking around at 9:30pm and the city was still basking in an evening glow.

#aazatravels

author: Adnan
caption:
The difference is night and day, literally, between the amount of people here at dawn vs dusk. So much more.plesant to actually explore Montmartre at this time and actually take it all in. The cobblestone streets, cute apartments, storefronts and even the lighting. If you get a chance to visit at dawn, do it!

#aazaadventures

author: Adnan
caption:
Morning. Early morning run with little to no sleep but still got all the way up the hill to Sacre Coeur. How are there people.here this morning??!?
I guess they stayed up. Mostly. (Notice the beer bottles?)
Anyways, the run was still.fun and it's nice and cool this early but I still had a good sweat going by the end. See my Montmartre video for a quick.selfie at the end.
#aazaadventures

author: Adnan
caption:
Today's adventure is on the Seine river in beautiful Paris! A one hour bateau ride in 2.5 mins. All the sights and a little history along the way. My favorite piece is the lovers bridge which was shut down in 2015 because of the weight of all the locks! 

But lovers will always find a way....

#aazaadventures

author: Amy
caption:
If it’s not the first question, it’s the second, “what are you going to do about school for the year?” We will be world schooling - learning about the world as we travel. There will be some online math, reading (because they love to), and writing (to record our travels and to share stories with our friends). We know that so much learning happens through experiences. We (the parents) will be learning along with the kids too. 

#aazatravels

author: Adnan
caption:
What's right with this photo?
#AAZAadventures #AAZAtravels

author: Adnan
caption:
First day of our year long travel adventure!
We begin in Paris, where I grew up. I'll be bringing the kids and @mackling to see the neighborhood I lived in and the school I attended. More importantly the learnings have already started with jetlag, taking care of the body and lessons in communication.
#AAZAadventures #AAZAtravels

author: Amy
caption:
It’s been a long time preparing for today - our anticipated year long journey traveling with the kids. We had to say a lot of (temporary) good byes. We feel sad to be far from family, especially my parents as we’ve lived next door to them for 5 years. We temporarily re-homed Ruby with one of her favorite people, Jeff and his wife, Jessica. We also packed up our home so we could rent out place for a year. Can’t believe it’s finally happening!

#aazatravels
